title: L01 · Alphabet & Umlauts
objective: Recognise every German letter by name, plus the four extras ä ö ü ß. You don't need to memorise — just hear each one once and notice which look familiar.
strategy: Tap the speaker icon. Listen, repeat aloud once, move on. The system tracks which letters you've heard. Use 'Play section' to run a whole group hands-free.

sections.title: The 26 standard letters
sections.note: German uses the same Latin letters as English and Spanish. The letter NAMES differ — that's what you're learning here.

blocks.hint: Long, open 'ah'. Like the 'a' in 'father'. Spanish: como la 'a' en 'padre'.
Same as English/Spanish 'b' at start of words. At END of a word it sounds like /p/.
Letter name is 'tseh'. The letter C alone is rare — usually appears in 'ch' or 'ck'.
Like English 'd'. At END of a word it sounds like /t/.
Long 'eh'. Like Spanish 'e' but slightly tenser. Don't say English 'ee'.
Letter name is 'eff'. Identical sound to English/Spanish 'f'.
Letter name is 'geh'. Always hard /g/, never soft like English 'gym'.
Letter name is 'hah'. Pronounced like English 'h' at the start of words; SILENT after a vowel where it just lengthens it.
Long 'ee'. Same as Spanish 'i'.
Letter name is 'yot'. Pronounced like English 'y' in 'yes' — NOT like English 'j'.
Identical to English/Spanish 'k'.
Lighter and clearer than English 'l' — closer to Spanish 'l'.
Identical to English/Spanish 'm'.
Identical to English/Spanish 'n'.
Long, rounded 'oh'. Like Spanish 'o' but lips slightly more rounded.
Like English/Spanish 'p' with a bit more breath at the start (aspirated).
Always followed by 'u' and pronounced /kv/ as a combo. The letter name alone is 'koo'.
German 'r' is in the THROAT (uvular), not rolled like Spanish. At the end of a word, it often softens to a vowel-like sound.
BEFORE a vowel: voiced like English 'z'. ELSEWHERE: like English 's'.
Like English 't', a bit more breath at the start.
Long, deep 'oo'. Like Spanish 'u'.
Letter name is 'fau'. In German words, V is pronounced /f/ — like English 'f', NOT 'v'.
This is the letter that sounds like English 'v'. Letter name is 'veh'.
Rare. Pronounced /ks/ like English/Spanish.
Letter name is 'üpsilon'. In loanwords mostly behaves like 'i' or 'ü'.
Letter name is 'tset'. Z always sounds like /ts/ — never /z/!

sections.title: The three Umlauts: ä ö ü
sections.note: Umlauts are vowels with two dots that change the sound. They are SEPARATE letters, not decorations. If you can't type them, write 'ae', 'oe', 'ue' as a fallback (but learn the real form).

blocks.hint: Sounds like the 'e' in English 'bed' but held longer. Spanish: parecido a la 'e' en 'café'.
Round your lips like you're saying 'o', but try to say 'e' instead. No exact English or Spanish equivalent — practice this.
Round your lips for 'oo', but say 'ee'. Like French 'u'. Spanish: NO equivalente directo — practica con los labios fruncidos.

sections.title: The Eszett: ß
sections.note: The ß (called 'Eszett' or 'scharfes S') is a single letter that sounds like 'ss'. It appears AFTER long vowels or diphthongs. You may also see 'ss' instead in Swiss German.

blocks.hint: Always pronounced like a sharp 's'. Never starts a word — only mid or end. If unavailable, write 'ss' (e.g. 'Strasse' for 'Straße').

sections.title: Hear the letters in real words
sections.note: Short sentences that use the trickier letters. Listen to the rhythm — notice how 'sp' and 'st' at the start become 'shp' and 'sht'.

blocks.translation: We live in Vienna. / Vivimos en Viena.
blocks.hint: Three W's in a row — all sound like English 'v'.

blocks.translation: Ten trains are running at the moment. / Diez trenes circulan ahora.
blocks.hint: Every Z is /ts/. Don't confuse with English Z.

blocks.translation: Girls like sweet bread rolls. / A las niñas les gustan los panecillos dulces.
blocks.hint: All three Umlauts plus an ß in one sentence.

blocks.translation: Yes, I'm called Jonas. / Sí, me llamo Jonas.
blocks.hint: J = English 'y' sound. Two of them here.
